schedule.notes: Week 16 Day 3 of Talk 11 (Week 3 of 3, Multi-Week Talk). Authority, Twaddle, and Reason-Respecting focus: three tendencies that shape communication and decision-making. Core frameworks: Authority-Misinfluence (biological programming to obey authority, even when wrong), Twaddle Tendency (born to prattle, wasteful talking over listening), Reason-Respecting (compliance with any reason, even bad ones). 5 actionable takeaways: audit authority incentives, create 'Meeting Twaddle Audit', design anti-obedience systems, give good reasons but recognize manipulation, document evidence before accepting authority decisions. 5 case snapshots: Milgram experiment (65% administer lethal shocks due to authority), Xerox machine study (94% compliance with bad reason), corporate meeting culture (twaddle destroying productivity), Berkshire culture (anti-twaddle, concise decisions), Inversion case (designing blind obedience). Mental model drill on Authority/Twaddle/Reason-Respecting with Day 3 prompts. 3 reflection questions: audit of expert advice acceptance, workplace authority and twaddle analysis, quality vs form of reasons evaluation. Personal commitment: identify one recurring 'twaddle' meeting and cancel or redesign it. Today's tendencies show social/communication software bugs that undermine judgment even with healthy brains. Next: Week 16 Day 4 - Reason-Respecting Deep Dive (Tendency #24) and Lollapalooza (#25). Message delivered successfully.

schedule.talkTitle: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations
schedule.notes: Week 8 Day 1 of Talk 6 (Week 1 of 1, Single-Week Talk). Read & Overview focus: 3% cost problem, drift toward complexity, why foundations destroy charitable capital through avoidable costs. Core frameworks: 3% Cost Problem (arithmetic of costs destroying wealth in low-return environments), Three-Layer Consultant Incentives (perverse structures adding cost and misalignment), Concentration vs. Diversification Trade-off (rational concentration when you understand the business). 5 actionable takeaways: calculate total cost drag, audit for layered complexity, question professional complexity, apply cost-return arithmetic test, recognize overconfidence trap. 5 case snapshots: LTCM collapse (lollapalooza effect of overconfidence + sophistication + leverage), GM fourth door mistake (professionalism over observation), Woodruff Foundation Coca-Cola concentration (wisdom of concentration through understanding), Berkshire low-cost approach (simplicity beats complexity), Fund of Funds revival (social proof recreating failed past models). Mental model drill on Inversion with Day 1 prompts: biggest insight challenging current thinking (professionalism doesn't equal better results), assumption Munger would question (diversification and professional management being inherently good). 3 reflection questions: audit of total investment costs, workplace professionalism complexity analysis, latticework building for incentive-caused bias/overconfidence/social proof/inversion. Personal commitment: calculate total annual investment costs as percentage of principal. Talk 6 focuses on foundation investment practices, showing how incentive-caused bias, overconfidence, and social proof lead foundations to adopt costly, complex consultant structures that destroy charitable capital. Next: Week 8 Day 2 - Mental Models (Incentive-Caused Bias, Overconfidence, Social Proof, Man-with-a-Hammer). Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Incentive-Caused Bias, Man-with-a-Hammer, Overconfidence, Social Proof, Lollapalooza
schedule.notes: Week 8 Day 2 of Talk 6 (Mental Models Focus). Four mental models: Incentive-Caused Bias (three-layer consultant structure creating perverse incentives, GM's fourth door mistake), Man-with-a-Hammer Syndrome (foundations copying Yale's complex fund-of-funds, LTCM collapse as lollapalooza effect), Overconfidence Bias (90% of Swedish drivers rate above average, investment professionals overestimate abilities, Feynman's self-criticism principle), Social Proof (Yale model contagion through institutions, Demosthenes quote 'What a man wishes, he will believe', consultant feedback loop). Lollapalooza Effect defined: multiple biases reinforcing each other (incentives + overconfidence + social proof + availability + liking + authority). Arithmetic: 17% - 3% costs - 5% donations = 9% net growth (good times); 5% - 3% costs - 5% donations = -3% shrinkage (bad times). Foundations lose 3% annually in bad markets. Berkshire's approach: <0.1% costs, zero turnover, few positions, deep understanding. Key takeaways: structure incentives first, question social proof, recognize overconfidence, watch for lollapalooza, simplicity beats complexity. Reflection questions: if consultant wanted max impact/min cost, what would they recommend? What professional confidence might be unwarranted? What are you doing 'because everyone else does it'? Are multiple forces pushing you toward complexity? Next: Week 8 Day 3 - Inversion. Message delivered successfully.

schedule.talkTitle: Inversion: How to Destroy Foundation Wealth
schedule.focus: Inversion Thinking, How to Destroy, Feynman's Self-Criticism Principle, Arithmetic of Disaster
schedule.notes: Week 8 Day 3 of Talk 6 (Inversion Focus). Inversion thinking: how to guarantee failure, then avoid that path. How to destroy foundation wealth: 5% returns - 3% costs - 5% donations = -3% annual shrinkage. 26% destroyed over 10 years, 45% destroyed over 20 years. How to create professional disaster: overconfidence + sophistication + leverage + no self-criticism (GM fourth door, LTCM collapse). How to waste charitable capital: layered consultants, high turnover, blind copying, no self-criticism. The croupier's take: investment costs destroying wealth. Feynman's self-criticism principle: 'You must not fool yourself—and you're the easiest person to fool.' How to avoid fooling yourself: seek disconfirming evidence, challenge assumptions, have trusted advisors challenge your thinking. Solution: eliminate complex consultant layers (1 layer instead of 3), reduce turnover to near zero, choose simplicity (indexing OR concentration, not middle ground), accept realistic returns (5%, not 17%), maintain self-criticism (Feynman principle), align incentives (single layer). Solution arithmetic: reduce costs from 3% to 1% or <0.1% (Berkshire). Bad markets: 5% - 1% - 5% = -1% shrinkage (vs -3%), Good markets: 17% - 1% - 5% = 11% net growth (vs 9%). Berkshire's costs <0.1%: 5% - 0.1% - 5% = -0.1% shrinkage, 17% - 0.1% - 5% = 11.9% net growth. Simplicity as virtue: complexity is expensive (3% cost drag), simplicity is effective (<0.1% costs, more capital retained), middle ground is worst (high costs, poor returns). Key takeaways: use inversion (ask how to guarantee failure, then avoid it), apply Feynman's self-criticism (seek disconfirming evidence), embrace simplicity (avoid complexity), align incentives (single layer). Reflection questions: if foundation returns 5%, how much grows after costs? What professional decisions have overconfidence + no self-criticism? What assumptions might be wrong? How would you guarantee failure in your work? If you save 2% costs annually, how much good over 20 years? Next: Week 8 Day 4 - Career Applications. Message delivered successfully.

Week 10 Day 1 of Talk 8 (Week 1 of 1, Single-Week Talk). Read & Overview focus: Munger's prophetic warning about accounting practices that would trigger Enron/WorldCom era scandals. Core frameworks: Stock Option Accounting Anomaly (GAAP loophole where options are tax-deductible but not expensed on income statement), 'Bit by Bit' Fraud Technique (gradual distortions accumulate, avoiding detection), Earnings Smoothing Incentive (analysts reward smooth growth, creating perverse incentives). 5 actionable takeaways: read stock options footnote first, question 'too smooth' earnings, avoid buyback + option combos, check auditor tenure, calculate true economic earnings. 5 case snapshots: Quant Tech fictional case (founder vs. successor eras), Enron (Munger's prediction confirmed), WorldCom (capitalizing expenses), Berkshire (anti-Quant Tech), contemporary tech startups (Quant Tech reborn). Mental model drill on Incentive-Caused Bias with Day 1 prompts: biggest insight challenging current thinking (smooth earnings != good management), assumption Munger would question (trust in audited statements). 3 reflection questions: audit of stock options footnote reading, workplace incentive alignment analysis, latticework building for incentives across domains. Personal commitment: read stock-based compensation footnote of one company. Talk 8 focuses on accounting fraud and stock option manipulation, showing how incentives, flawed accounting rules, and human psychology combine to create predictable financial disasters. Next: Week 10 Day 2 - Mental Models (Incentive-Caused Bias, Inversion, Inconsistency-Avoidance, Man-with-a-Hammer). Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Incentive-Caused Bias, Man-with-a-Hammer, Overconfidence, Social Proof, Lollapalooza

Lollapalooza Effect defined: multiple biases reinforcing each other (incentives + overconfidence + social proof + availability + liking + authority). Arithmetic: 17% - 3% costs - 5% donations = 9% net growth (good times); 5% - 3% costs - 5% donations = -3% shrinkage (bad times). Foundations lose 3% annually in bad markets. schedule.focus: Better Approaches to Economic Thinking (Multidisciplinary Economics)
schedule.notes: Week 12 Day 1 of Talk 9 (Week 2 of 2, Multi-Week Talk). Better Approaches focus: Multidisciplinary economics, scenario thinking vs forecasting, circle of competence applied to economics. Core frameworks: Multidisciplinary Economics (combine insights from psychology, biology, history, practical business experience), Scenario Thinking vs Forecasting (build systems robust across scenarios instead of predicting), Circle of Competence Applied to Economics (recognize limits of knowledge and complexity). 5 actionable takeaways: add psychological layers to every economic analysis, replace forecasts with scenario analysis, practice physics envy detection, build economic latticework, prioritize rough-but-right over precise-but-wrong. 5 case snapshots: Berkshire vs EMH (beating the market for decades), LTCM collapse (Nobel laureates failed due to physics envy), 2008 Financial Crisis (models ignored incentives and psychology), Market Bubbles Throughout History (psychology trumps models), Inversion Case (forecasting trap - confidence kills). Mental model drill on Multidisciplinary Economics with Day 1 prompts: biggest insight challenging current thinking (elegance doesn't equal accuracy), assumption Munger would question (reliance on economic models/forecasts). 3 reflection questions: audit of economic analysis for flawed assumptions, workplace incentives for false confidence in predictions, latticework building across disciplines. Personal commitment: audit one economic analysis/forecast by adding psychological factors and questioning assumptions. Talk 9 Week 2 focuses on constructive solutions to economic thinking problems: incorporate psychology, accept uncertainty, use multidisciplinary mental models. Next: Week 12 Day 2 - Mental Models for Economic Analysis. Message delivered successfully.

schedule.talkTitle: USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address
schedule.notes: Week 13 Day 1 of Talk 10 (Week 1 of 1, Single-Week Talk). Read & Overview focus: Munger's most personal and practical talk - 84 years of wisdom for entering professionals. Core frameworks: Deserve What You Want (trust-building principle), Learning Machine (continuous knowledge acquisition), Seamless Web of Deserved Trust (civilization model). 5 actionable takeaways: deliver what you would buy, choose employers you admire, avoid unreliability, practice assiduity, recognize self-serving bias. 5 case snapshots: Max Planck vs Chauffeur (surface vs deep knowledge), John Wooden's non-egalitarian coaching, Mozart's financial mismanagement (self-serving bias), Epictetus's response to setbacks, two-man partnership's assiduity rule. Mental model drill on Seamless Web of Deserved Trust with Day 1 prompts: biggest insight (success comes from earning merit, not shortcuts), assumption (bureaucracy is necessary vs symptom of missing trust). 3 reflection questions: audit recent work quality, examine trust in current environment, start daily learning habit. Personal commitment: apply 'deserve what you want' test to one piece of work. Talk 10 synthesizes themes from earlier talks but applies them personally - character traits, habits, and choices for life success. Next: Week 13 Day 2 - Mental Models (Deserve What You Want, Learning Machine, Seamless Web of Trust). Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Inversion Thinking — How Psychological Tendencies Destroy Decision-Making
schedule.notes: Week 14 Day 3 of Talk 11 (Week 1 of 3, Multi-Week Talk). Inversion Focus: Four paths to ruin from combining psychological tendencies. Core frameworks: The Ego Loop (Self-Regard + Overoptimism + Doubt-Avoidance + Inconsistency), The Social Trap (Social Proof + Reciprocation + Liking + Authority), The Loss Spiral (Deprival-Superreaction + Denial + Commitment + Stress), The Information Chamber (Availability + Contrast + Association + Reward). Lollapalooza Effect: Single tendency = 1x, Two tendencies = 10x, Three+ tendencies = 50-100x impact. Structural solutions: Pre-mortems, Designated Dissenters, Pre-committed Exits, Reality Checks, Lollapalooza Mapping. 5 actionable takeaways: Build pre-mortems into decision process, Appoint designated dissenters, Pre-commit exit rules before entering, Do weekly reality checks, Map Lollapalooza effects. 5 case snapshots: Theranos collapse (Ego + Denial + Authority), Ford Pinto disaster (Incentives + Denial + Authority), Moonies recruitment (Isolation + Liking + Commitment), Berkshire Hathaway culture (Anti-Lollapalooza), AI safety and Lollapalooza risk. 5 reflection questions: What was your last major decision? Write a pre-mortem. Who is your designated dissenter? What exit rules have you pre-committed? What echo chamber are you in? What Lollapalooza effects operate in your life? Personal commitment: Write a pre-mortem for one major upcoming decision. Key insight: You cannot eliminate psychological tendencies (they're hardwired), but you can design systems that break their destructive loops. Next: Week 14 Day 4 - Career Applications. Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Career Applications — How Psychological Tendencies Shape Work Decisions
schedule.notes: Week 14 Day 4 of Talk 11 (Week 1 of 3, Multi-Week Talk). Career & Professional Focus: Applying psychological tendencies to workplace scenarios. Core frameworks: Career Inversion Audit (write 'Prescription for Career Misery' before major decisions), Workplace Incentive Alignment (design systems that harness Reward Superresponse for good outcomes), Professional Doubt-Avoidance Detector (mental alarm system for quick decisions). 5 actionable takeaways: write 3-bullet 'Prescription for Career Misery' before major decisions, create 'Psychology Checklist' for job offers, apply Inversion to career ruin (overestimate abilities + rush decisions + refuse change + avoid challenge), design systems to break bad psychological loops, neutralize Liking/Disliking tendency by asking 'would you still choose this without knowing who is involved?'. 5 case snapshots: GM fourth door mistake (Inconsistency-Avoidance + Excessive Self-Regard), 2023 tech layoff herd (Social Proof + Availability), promotion based on liking (Liking/Loving + Excessive Self-Regard), Berkshire executive compensation design (harnessing Reward Superresponse), cult-like company culture (Lollapalooza: Authority + Social Proof + Commitment + Doubt-Avoidance). Mental model drill on Career Inversion: what would Munger advise differently in current work situation? how to apply these principles in next career decision? 5 reflection questions: audit of recent quick work decisions (Doubt-Avoidance), workplace incentive structures analysis, choose one tendency affecting career + countermeasure, last major career decision psychological audit, professional ethics scenario (promotion based on liking). Personal commitment: identify ONE psychological tendency influencing a current work decision and write a specific countermeasure. Next: Week 14 Day 5 - Psychology & Bias Focus. Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Week 1 Review – Synthesis of Incentive & Social Tendencies (#1–8)
schedule.notes: Week 14 Day 7 of Talk 11 (Week 1 of 3, Multi-Week Talk). Week 1 Review & Synthesis: Integration of all 8 foundational psychological tendencies (Reward Superresponse, Liking, Disliking, Doubt-Avoidance, Inconsistency, Curiosity, Kantian Fairness, Envy) into a unified framework. Core frameworks: The Eight Foundational Tendencies (complete list + hierarchy), Lollapalooza Effect (multiple tendencies combining for multiplied impact), Structural Countermeasures (environmental design to neutralize tendencies). 5 actionable takeaways: Map the 3 most active tendencies in your life for one week, run '8-Tendency Check' before major decisions, write personal 'Prescription for Decision Disaster' using inversion, audit environment for tendency triggers, create 'Personal Tendency Profile' with top 3 danger zones. 5 case snapshots: FedEx incentive shift (Reward Superresponse), Coca-Cola positive Lollapalooza (multiple tendencies working together), Real Estate agent contrast trap (Contrast-Misreaction), Theranos collapse (Lollapalooza of bad tendencies), Perfect investment disaster (8 tendencies aligned). Daily mental model drill: one permanent model to add to latticework, tendency radar development, identifying recent Lollapalooza effects. Week in review: You cannot eliminate tendencies (hardwired) but can design systems to neutralize them (checklists, pre-commitments, designated dissenters, pre-mortems). Integration exercise: Create Personal 'Tendency Radar' checklist for important decisions. Personal commitment: Identify ONE psychological tendency that influenced a recent decision and write what happened and what would be done differently next time. Next: Week 14 Day 8-10 - Week 1 Continuation (Tendencies 9-17). Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Week 2 Review – Synthesis of Cognitive & Emotional Biases (#9–17)
schedule.notes: Week 15 Day 7 of Talk 11 (Week 2 of 3, Multi-Week Talk). Week 2 Review & Synthesis: Integration of cognitive and emotional biases (#9–17) including Reciprocation, Association, Denial, Self-Regard, Overoptimism, Deprival-Superreaction, Social Proof, Contrast-Misreaction, and Stress-Influence. Core frameworks: Cognitive Distortion Layer (how minds misinterpret information), Self-Deception Loop (Denial + Self-Regard + Overoptimism), Lollapalooza Danger Zone (multiple tendencies aligning for multiplied impact). 5 actionable takeaways: map cognitive blind spots, practice Feynman's self-criticism principle, create pre-commitment rules for stress, neutralize social proof in decisions, use inversion on major decisions. 5 case snapshots: Open-Outcry Auctions (Deprival-Superreaction + Social Proof), Kitty Genovese case (Social Proof + Contrast), LTCM collapse (Excessive Self-Regard + Overoptimism), Real Estate agent price anchoring (Contrast-Misreaction), Panic selling in market crashes (Stress-Influence + Social Proof). Week in review: Tendencies #9–17 represent the cognitive and emotional distortion layer; cannot eliminate these tendencies (hardwired) but can design systems to neutralize them (structural solutions, checklists, pre-commitment rules, inversion thinking, Lollapalooza mapping). Personal commitment: Identify ONE of tendencies #9–17 that influenced a recent decision and write specific countermeasure. Next: Week 3 - Tendencies #18–25 + Full Synthesis. Message delivered successfully.

Week 16 Day 2 of Talk 11 (Week 3 of 3, Multi-Week Talk). Chemical & Age Effects focus: Drug-Misinfluence (#20) and Senescence-Misinfluence (#21) tendencies show how biological factors compromise judgment. Core frameworks: Cognitive Foundation Principle (brain is biological organ, performance depends on physical factors), Drug-Misinfluence Tendency (chemicals alter judgment directly), Senescence-Misinfluence Tendency (cognitive decline with age is inevitable but rate varies). 5 actionable takeaways: establish 'no major decisions' rule for substance use, track 'cognitive edge' score daily, practice continuous thinking to combat aging, create cognitive audit checklist, design impairment triggers into decision systems. 5 case snapshots: Three Things Catastrophe (drugs, liquor, leverage), Trading While Impaired, Retirement Brain, Munger's Continuous Learning, 'I'm Fine' Delusion. Mental model drill on Drug-Misinfluence and Senescence-Misinfluence with Day 2 prompts: cognitive audit, cognitive edge rating, aging preparation plan. 5 reflection questions: audit of recent compromised decisions, substance awareness rule, aging preparation plan, system design for catching impairment, Munger's cognitive maintenance habits. Personal commitment: rate mental sharpness (1–10) before decisions; if below 7, defer 24 hours. Today's tendencies show biological limits: no mental models can overcome a chemically or biologically compromised brain. Protect cognitive machinery as valuable asset. Next: Week 16 Day 3 - Authority, Twaddle, and Reason-Respecting (Tendencies #22–24). Message delivered successfully.
